1

Assignment 1 — Systematic Paper Analysis

A1

What is SIREN?

SIREN (Sinusoidal Representation Network) is a neural network architecture that uses periodic sine activations instead of ReLU, enabling faithful representation of complex continuous signals and PDE solving. Proposed by Sitzmann et al. at NeurIPS 2020, it addresses the fundamental failure of ReLU-based implicit networks: zero second derivatives make them incapable of capturing high-frequency content or satisfying physics-based constraints.

Phi(x) = W_L · (phi_{L-1} o phi_{L-2} o … o phi_0)(x) + b_L
where each layer: phi_i(x_i) = sin(omega_0 · (W_i x_i + b_i))
Weight init: W ~ U(-sqrt(6/n), sqrt(6/n)) for hidden layers
First layer scale: omega_0 = 30

Problem

ReLU MLPs have zero second derivatives everywhere. They cannot represent curvature, solve PDEs with derivative constraints, or faithfully capture high-frequency signal content like sharp textures, audio harmonics, or complex boundaries.

Key Insight

The derivative of a SIREN is itself a SIREN. Training on gradients or Laplacians directly requires no special treatment — the loss is applied to the network's own analytical derivative. No numerical solvers needed.

Solution

Replace all activations with sin(omega_0 · x) and use a principled uniform initialisation scheme to prevent vanishing/exploding gradients in deep periodic networks.

Activation Distributions in a SIREN

Figure 1. Activation distributions across layers in a SIREN. The principled uniform initialisation keeps the signal distribution constant across depth — preventing vanishing and exploding gradients that afflict conventional initialisations in periodic networks.

Architecture Details

Frequency Scaling ω₀ = 30

Scales input coordinates so the network captures high-frequency spatial/temporal data from the first layer. Without it, normalized inputs in [−1, 1] stay in the near-linear region of sine — producing an effectively linear model. The value 30 is empirically determined as the optimal balance between fine-detail representation and gradient stability.

Principled Initialisation

Weights sampled from U(−√(6/n), √(6/n)), derived to keep activation distributions constant throughout network depth. This is essential: Xavier and He initialisation schemes introduce vanishing gradients in periodic networks, making deep SIRENs impossible to train without this scheme.

Datasets Used

ModalityDataset / SignalTask
ImagesBSDS500 — starfish photo (256×256)Image fitting, Poisson reconstruction
AudioBach cello suite (7 s), Counting clip (12 s) at 44,100 HzWaveform fitting
VideoCat video (300 frames), bikes sequenceVideo fitting (x, y, t → RGB)
3D ShapesStanford Thai Statue (500K surface points)Signed Distance Function (SDF)
PDEs2D synthetic domainsHelmholtz, Wave, Eikonal equations
FacesCelebA (32×32)Hypernetwork prior over distributions

Audio Fitting Result

Figure 2. Audio waveform fitting on Bach’s Cello Suite. Left (SIREN): ground truth, predicted output, and error — nearly identical. Right (ReLU): predicted output is nearly flat — complete failure to capture audio harmonics. Only two audio clips were tested in the entire paper.

Baselines Compared

ReLU MLP
Standard MLP
Tanh
Smooth activation
Softplus
Smooth activation
ReLU+PE
Positional Encoding (NeRF)
DeepSDF
Specialised 3D baseline

Critical Analysis

!

No principled way to choose ω₀. The value ω₀ = 30 was chosen by trial and error. For audio, the paper silently changes the input domain from [−1, 1] to [−100, 100] — equivalent to multiplying ω₀ by 100. NeurIPS Reviewer 3 raised this concern; the authors tested robustness only on a single image task.

!

Training is extremely slow — one network per signal. Training a single image takes ~90 min; a single 3D shape takes ~6 h; the wave equation takes ~25 h. Scaling to even 100 images would require hundreds of GPU-hours.

TaskIterationsTime (per signal)
Image fitting (Poisson)10,000~90 min
3D shape (SDF)50,000~6 hours
Helmholtz equation50,000~8 hours
Full-waveform inversion90,000~27 hours
Wave equation100,000~25 hours
!

Thin evaluation. Images: 1 photograph. Audio: 2 clips. Video: 1 cat video. 3D shapes: 2 objects. PDEs: all 2D synthetic only. No statistically meaningful comparison over large benchmarks.

Proposed Research Opportunities

Extension 1 — Diverse Image Evaluation

Evaluate on full BSDS500 (500 images) and a medical dataset (ChestX-ray14 / Brain MRI). Report mean ± std of PSNR, gradient-PSNR, and Laplacian-PSNR — the first statistically meaningful comparison of SIREN across image categories.

Extension 2 — Adaptive Frequency Scaling

Make ω₀ a learnable per-layer parameter, initialised from FFT-estimated dominant frequency. Adds just 1 scalar per hidden layer with no other architectural changes. Could resolve the ω₀ inconsistency across tasks.

Extension 3 — Knowledge Distillation

Distil a 5-layer 256-unit teacher into a 3-layer 64-unit student, matching both signal outputs and intermediate activations. 12× parameter reduction with expected 1–3 dB PSNR drop — competitive with JPEG/MP3.

A2
i

Objective: Reproduce all six original SIREN experiments from the official GitHub repository under constrained hardware (T4 14.56 GB, RTX 3050 4 GB), fixing 8 bugs discovered in the original codebase.

Valeena — Image Fitting

Image Fitting (Cameraman 512×512)

All five model types (SIREN, ReLU, Tanh, ReLU-PE/NeRF, RBF) trained on a 512×512 cameraman image for 10,000 steps. PSNR recorded from TensorBoard at step 9,000. RBF stopped at 1,000 steps due to GPU memory limits on the 4 GB RTX 3050.

Figure 3. Image training loss (left) and PSNR curves (right) across all five models. SIREN converges fastest and reaches the highest PSNR. RBF stopped at step 1,000 due to OOM on the 4 GB GPU.
Figure 4. Ground truth (left) vs. NeRF/ReLU-PE predicted image (right) at step 9,000. Structural content is preserved; fine detail is slightly softer than the paper due to fewer training steps.
Paper (Original)

SIREN: ~50 dB at 15,000 steps

ReLU-PE (NeRF): ~38 dB

RBF: ~29 dB

ReLU: ~25 dB

Tanh: ~26 dB

Our Reproduction

SIREN: 40.37 dB at 10,000 steps

ReLU-PE (NeRF): 31.11 dB

RBF: 23.50 dB (1,000 steps — OOM)

ReLU: 23.99 dB

Tanh: 21.91 dB

ModelPaper PSNRReproduced PSNRGapSteps
SIREN~50 dB40.37 dB−9.6 dB10,000
ReLU PE (NeRF)~38 dB31.11 dB−6.9 dB10,000
RBF~29 dB23.50 dB−5.5 dB1,000 (OOM)
ReLU~25 dB23.99 dB−1.0 dB10,000
Tanh~26 dB21.91 dB−4.1 dB10,000
+

Model ranking preserved exactly: SIREN > ReLU-PE > RBF ≈ ReLU > Tanh. PSNR gaps are directly attributable to fewer training steps — the paper shows SIREN still improving at step 10,000 of 15,000.

Valeena — Audio

Audio Fitting (Bach Cello Suite & Counting Clip)

SIREN and ReLU trained on Bach cello suite (7 s) and a counting clip (12 s) at 44,100 Hz. Input domain [−100, 100] as used in the paper.

Figure 5. Audio training loss for all four runs. SIREN Bach converges to near-zero; SIREN Counting stopped early at step 1,676. ReLU runs plateau at substantially higher loss.
Figure 6. Audio waveform reproductions — Bach SIREN (left: GT / predicted / error) and Counting SIREN (right). Bach: near-perfect reconstruction; Counting: partial convergence due to early stop.
Clip / ModelPaper MSEReproduced MSESteps
Bach — SIREN1.101×10&sup5;~0 (converged)9,000
Bach — ReLU2.504×10²2.48×10²9,000
Counting — SIREN3.816×10&sup4;5.0×10&sup4;1,676 (early stop)
Counting — ReLU7.466×10³7.5×10³9,000
+

Bach SIREN reached near-zero MSE — perfect reconstruction. ReLU Bach MSE matches paper within 1%. ReLU produces a near-flat waveform output, confirming the paper’s audio claim.

Valeena — Video

Video Fitting (Bikes Sequence 272×640)

SIREN and ReLU trained on the bikes sequence from scikit-video. SIREN exceeded the paper’s PSNR despite training for only ~1.8% of the paper’s total steps.

Figure 7. Video PSNR (left) and loss (right). SIREN converges rapidly and surpasses the paper’s PSNR at step 1,809. ReLU lags significantly with fewer steps.
ModelPaper PSNRReproduced PSNRGapSteps
SIREN29.90 dB30.70 dB+0.80 dB (exceeded)1,809
ReLU25.12 dB19.81 dB−5.31 dB1,958
+

SIREN exceeded the paper’s PSNR by +0.80 dB in under 1,900 steps — 1.8% of the paper’s 100K training budget. This demonstrates SIREN’s exceptional convergence speed on video.

Laiba — PDE

Helmholtz Equation

The Helmholtz equation ∇²u + k²n²u = −q models wave propagation in a heterogeneous medium. SIREN minimizes the PDE residual over 2D spatial coordinates, outputting a complex wavefield (real + imaginary parts).

Figure 1. Helmholtz loss curve — 5,581,349 → 175,564 (96.9% reduction). Large absolute values are expected: loss is summed (not averaged) over all sampled points, confirmed in GitHub Issue #7.
MetricPaper (Original)Our Reproduction
Training Steps50,0005,000
Initial LossNot reported5,581,349
Final LossNot reported175,564
Loss Reduction~96%+ (visual convergence)96.9%
Training Time / Hardware~8 hrs, 24 GB GPU~63 min, T4 14.56 GB
Laiba — PDE

Wave Equation

The wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∂²u/∂x² uses 3D input (x, y, t) and outputs scalar displacement u(x, t) over the spacetime domain.

Figure 2 (left). Wave loss — 85,955,296 → 16,236 (99.98% reduction).
Figure 2 (right). Spacetime solution u(x, t) and amplitude |u(x, t)| — structured wave patterns confirm successful convergence.
MetricPaperReproduced
Training Steps100,0005,000
Initial LossNot reported85,955,296
Final LossNot reported16,236
Loss ReductionNot reported99.98%
Hidden Units / Time512 / ~25 hrs256 (OOM fix) / ~67 min
Maryam — PDE

Poisson Equation

SIREN reconstructs the cameraman image (256×256, BSD500) using only derivative supervision — no direct pixel values. Two variants: gradient supervision and Laplacian supervision, each for 10,000 steps with batch size 16,384.

Figure 8. Poisson reconstruction results. Top row (L→R): Ground truth, gradient-supervised SIREN (PSNR 33.56 dB), Laplacian-supervised SIREN (PSNR 11.87 dB), absolute difference map. Bottom row: GT gradient x/y components, GT magnitude, predicted gradient magnitude.
Figure 9. Poisson training loss curves — gradient supervision (blue, final 0.2330) vs. Laplacian supervision (red, final 1836.97). Both converge smoothly; gradient supervision reaches substantially lower loss as expected for the less ill-posed problem.
MetricGradient SupervisionLaplacian Supervision
PSNR — Ours33.56 dB (+0.65 vs paper)11.87 dB
PSNR — Paper32.91 dB14.95 dB
MSE — Ours0.0004400.064949
Final Loss0.23301836.97
+

Gradient supervision exceeded paper PSNR by +0.65 dB. Both variants confirm SIREN can reconstruct images from derivative supervision alone — no direct pixel values required.

Maryam — 3D Shape

Signed Distance Function (SDF)

SIREN learns an SDF satisfying |∇Φ|=1 (Eikonal constraint) from oriented point clouds. The Stanford Thai Statue was replaced with a trimesh synthetic sphere (500K surface points) due to download and compute constraints on Colab.

Figure 10. SDF reconstruction using SIREN. Top: Four 3D views of the reconstructed sphere mesh (marching cubes, 75,867 vertices, 151,712 faces). Bottom: XY/XZ/YZ signed distance field cross-sections (blue=interior, red=exterior, white contour=zero level set). Bottom-right: Training loss curve converging to 2.914.
MetricPaper (Thai Statue)Reproduced (Sphere)
Training Steps50,0005,000
Loss at ConvergenceNot reported2.9136
Extracted Vertices / FacesNot reported75,867 / 151,712
Eikonal |∇Φ|≈1SatisfiedSatisfied

Assignment 2 — Conclusion

All six SIREN experiments reproduced successfully. The central claim — periodic sine activations outperform ReLU-based baselines on signal fitting and PDE solving — is confirmed in every experiment. Model ranking preserved in image fitting; audio MSE within 1%; video SIREN exceeded paper PSNR by +0.80 dB in under 2,000 steps; Poisson gradient exceeded paper PSNR by +0.65 dB; SDF correctly satisfied the Eikonal constraint; both PDE loss curves showed rapid, stable convergence.

3

Assignment 3 — Experimentation & Expansion

A3
i

Objective: Extend SIREN with four original improvements — testing new loss functions, activation variants, systematic ablations, and a novel cross-domain application not present in the original paper.

Valeena — Improvement I

CAOS-SIREN — Adaptive ω₀ Scheduling

Modified SIREN to use a Cosine Annealed Omega Schedule (CAOS) — ω₀ anneals over 10,000 steps rather than remaining fixed at 30. Two ranges tested: a narrow 25→45 and a wide 10→60.

CAOS-SIREN PSNR training curves. The PSNR rises then falls — confirming that increasing ω₀ mid-training destabilizes learned frequency representations.
Omega schedule over training steps for both CAOS runs.
Modelω₀ RangePSNRvs Baseline (40.37 dB)Status
SIREN A2 BaselineFixed 3040.37 dB—Reference
CAOS-SIREN Run 110 → 6029.77 dB−10.60 dBFailed
CAOS-SIREN Run 225 → 4519.59 dB−20.78 dBFailed
!

Finding: Dynamic ω₀ scheduling destabilizes SIREN’s initialization scheme. When ω₀ increases mid-training the initialization assumptions break down, causing gradient instability. SIREN’s strength lies in its principled initialization rather than training dynamics.

Valeena — Improvement II

Fourier Frequency Loss (FFL)

Added Fourier Frequency Loss to standard L2: L_total = L2 + λ × ||FFT(pred) − FFT(gt)||⊂1 with three λ values: {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}.

FFL loss curves. The λ=0.1 curve shows instability — rises then partially drops. The FFT loss term dominates L2 even with normalization.
Improvement III: Gradient loss curves for all three λ values with 1,000-step warmup.
ModelPSNRFinal Lossvs BaselineStatus
SIREN Baseline40.37 dB——Reference
FFL λ=0.0129.37 dB0.0387−11.00 dBFailed
FFL λ=0.0526.77 dB0.1745−13.60 dBFailed
FFL λ=0.125.77 dB0.3541−14.60 dBFailed

Improvement III — Gradient Loss

ModelPSNRSSIMvs BaselineStatus
SIREN Baseline40.37 dB——Reference
GradLoss λ=0.0126.14 dB0.59−14.23 dBFailed (closest)
GradLoss λ=0.0521.48 dB0.38−18.89 dBFailed
GradLoss λ=0.121.47 dB0.31−18.90 dBFailed
All Improvements I–III: PSNR comparison across CAOS-SIREN, FFL, and Gradient Loss variants relative to the A2 baseline (40.37 dB).
!

Key Insight: All three proposed improvements resulted in lower PSNR than the baseline. SIREN’s fixed ω₀=30 initialization and pure L2 loss are already highly optimized. Any modification disrupts the carefully designed initialization scheme. Improvements should target architecture changes rather than loss functions or scheduling.

Maryam — Improvement III

H-SIREN Activation + Stanford Bunny SDF

H-SIREN modifies the first layer activation from sin(ω₀x) to sin(sinh(2x)), potentially broadening the frequency range at the start of training. Evaluated on Poisson image reconstruction and SDF learning on the Stanford Bunny.

Figure 4.2.3 (Row 1). SIREN SDF cross-sections for the Stanford Bunny (XY, XZ, YZ planes). The white contour is the zero level set — a valid, well-defined surface is reconstructed.
Figure 4.2.3 (Row 2). H-SIREN SDF cross-sections for the same Stanford Bunny. The slices are empty — the network collapsed to a constant function and did not converge to an SDF.
SIREN training loss curve — Stanford Bunny SDF.
H-SIREN training loss curve — stalled at 1.51e−01.
SDF comparison summary plots.
ModelImagePSNRMSEFinal Loss
SIRENCameraman33.46 dB0.0004511.00e+00
H-SIRENCameraman33.41 dB0.0004563.47e+00
SIRENCoffee31.94 dB0.0006402.94e−01
H-SIRENCoffee26.83 dB0.0020769.11e+01
ModelShapeVerticesFacesFinal LossResult
SIRENStanford Bunny50,083100,1564.16e+00Valid SDF
H-SIRENStanford Bunny401,006*876,634*1.51e−01Collapsed to constant

* H-SIREN collapsed — high vertex count reflects a degenerate mesh, not reconstruction quality.

!

Finding: H-SIREN matches SIREN on simple images (−0.05 dB on cameraman) but diverges on complex tasks. The sin(sinh(2x)) activation is an improvement only when paired with a suitable initialization, which has not yet been derived.

Laiba — Improvement IV-A

Helmholtz Equation — k-Wavenumber Ablation

The original paper trains with a single fixed wavenumber k. This experiment systematically ablates k ∈ {1, 5, 20, 50} to study how SIREN convergence and wavefield complexity vary with wavenumber — a question not addressed in the original paper.

Figure 1. Helmholtz loss curves for k ∈ {1, 5, 20, 50}. k=50 shows periodic instability spikes while still converging. Dashed line = A2 final loss at 5K steps (175,564). Centre: Final loss at 1K steps. Right: Loss reduction — all four k values achieve 100% reduction.
Figure 2. Wavefield outputs (real part u_r, imaginary part u_i, amplitude |u|) for each k value. As k increases, spatial oscillation frequency grows — k=1 produces a near-uniform field, k=50 produces a dense, high-frequency interference pattern consistent with physical expectations.
Figure 3. Left: Normalised loss curves — all k values collapse to the same convergence trajectory after normalisation. Right: Steps to 95% loss reduction — k=5 is fastest (14 steps), k=50 slowest (22 steps).
k ValueInitial LossFinal Loss (1K steps)Reduction %Steps to 95%
k=18,543,346261100.0%15
k=56,551,798166100.0%14 (fastest)
k=2017,054,916586100.0%19
k=50107,369,63224,346100.0%22 (slowest)
k=1 (A2, 5K steps)5,581,349175,56496.9%—
+

Key Finding: SIREN converges for all k values (k=1 to k=50) achieving 100% loss reduction in all cases within 1,000 steps. Wavefield complexity scales predictably with k. The original paper’s single-k evaluation under-represents SIREN’s frequency range capability.

Laiba — Improvement IV-B

Wave Equation — Initial Condition Variation

The original paper fixes a single initial condition IC-1: u(x,0) = sin(πx). This experiment adds IC-2 (Gaussian pulse) and IC-3 (double sinusoid) to study whether SIREN convergence depends on the initial displacement profile.

Figure 4. Loss curves for all three ICs. IC-2 (Gaussian) converges fastest and to the lowest final loss. IC-3 (Double-sin) converges slowest to the highest final loss.
Figure 6. The three initial displacement profiles. IC-1: single sinusoid (baseline). IC-2: compact Gaussian pulse. IC-3: superposition of two frequencies.
Figure 5. Spacetime solutions u(x, t) (top row) and amplitude |u(x, t)| (bottom row) for each IC. All three produce structured wave patterns confirming successful SIREN convergence. IC-2 produces the most spatially focused wave propagation; IC-3 produces a richer multi-modal amplitude pattern.
Initial ConditionTypeInitial LossFinal Loss (500 steps)Reduction %
IC-1: sin(πx)Baseline71,59842399.4%
IC-2: exp(−||x||²/0.05)Gaussian pulse56,604116 (best)99.8%
IC-3: sin(2πx)+0.5sin(4πx)Double sinusoid70,266492 (highest)99.3%
+

Key Finding: SIREN converges for all three ICs. IC-2 Gaussian achieves the lowest final loss (116 vs 423 for IC-1, 99.8% reduction). All three produce structured, physically plausible spacetime wave solutions.

Laiba — Bonus

Brain MRI Super-Resolution (IXI T1 Dataset)

Novel cross-domain application: sagittal T1-weighted brain MRI slices from the IXI dataset are downsampled 2× and 4×. SIREN, trained on low-resolution pixel coordinates, is evaluated at full HR resolution. Baselines: bicubic interpolation and ReLU MLP. Also tested: SIREN+FFL+CAOS combining Improvements I and II.

Figure 7. Visual comparison for 2× SR. Columns: HR ground truth | LR input (upsampled) | Bicubic (26.78 dB / 0.894) | ReLU MLP (19.17 dB / 0.422) | SIREN-A2 (25.98 dB / 0.878) | SIREN+FFL+CAOS (14.37 dB / 0.083). SIREN is competitive with bicubic, preserving structural anatomy. SIREN+FFL+CAOS produces noisy output — the combined modification is unstable in this cross-domain setting.
Figure 8. Quantitative MRI SR results at 2× (top) and 4× (bottom) scale across 5 slices (mean ± std). SIREN (A2) closely matches bicubic at 2× (26.33 vs 26.99 dB PSNR) and remains competitive at 4× (21.40 vs 22.29 dB). ReLU MLP lags behind both. SIREN+FFL+CAOS fails in this domain (14.20 dB).
MethodScalePSNR (dB)SSIMNotes
Bicubic2×26.99 ± 0.540.8829 ± 0.007Traditional baseline
ReLU MLP2×19.31 ± 0.470.3721 ± 0.043A2 baseline
SIREN (A2)2×26.33 ± 0.550.8645 ± 0.007Within 0.66 dB of bicubic
SIREN+FFL+CAOS2×14.20 ± 0.900.1013 ± 0.013Unstable
Bicubic4×22.29 ± 0.560.6245 ± 0.015Traditional baseline
ReLU MLP4×19.05 ± 0.550.3265 ± 0.028A2 baseline
SIREN (A2)4×21.40 ± 0.600.5486 ± 0.018Competitive at 4×
SIREN+FFL+CAOS4×13.33 ± 1.000.0555 ± 0.011Unstable
+

Key Finding (SIREN): SIREN achieves 26.33 dB PSNR / 0.8645 SSIM at 2× brain MRI super-resolution — within 0.66 dB of bicubic, substantially outperforming ReLU MLP (19.31 dB). SIREN generalises to medical imaging without any domain-specific modification.

!

Key Finding (SIREN+FFL+CAOS): The combined variant fails (14.20 dB, SSIM 0.10). The Fourier Frequency Loss conflicts with the smooth, low-contrast nature of MRI intensity distributions. This meaningful negative result shows that improvements validated on natural images do not automatically transfer to medical imaging domains.

Assignment 3 — Conclusion

Four original improvements were developed and tested. SIREN’s fixed ω₀=30 initialization and pure L2 loss proved highly optimized — all three loss and scheduling modifications failed to improve upon it. H-SIREN matches SIREN on simple images but collapses on SDF tasks due to breaking the initialization scheme. Laiba’s k-ablation showed SIREN handles wavenumbers k=1 through k=50 with 100% loss reduction in all cases, extending the original paper’s single-k evaluation. The MRI SR bonus confirms SIREN generalises to medical imaging (26.33 dB PSNR, within 0.66 dB of bicubic) without any modification.

Key Takeaways

  • SIREN’s central claim is confirmed: periodic sine activations outperform ReLU-based baselines on all signal types and PDE tasks.
  • Model ranking in image fitting is preserved exactly; audio MSE matches within 1%; video SIREN exceeded paper PSNR by 0.80 dB in 1.8% of the paper’s training budget.
  • All modifications to the frequency schedule or loss function hurt performance — SIREN’s strength is its principled initialization, not its training dynamics.
  • H-SIREN works comparably on simple images but fails on SDF tasks by breaking the initialization scheme required by the Eikonal loss.
  • SIREN handles Helmholtz wavenumbers k=1 through k=50 with 100% loss reduction; the original paper tested only a single k value.
  • SIREN generalises to brain MRI super-resolution without modification, achieving near-bicubic PSNR (26.33 vs 26.99 dB at 2×).

Based on: Sitzmann et al., “Implicit Neural Representations with Periodic Activation Functions,” NeurIPS 2020.
